description: PHP baseline invariants — deliberately narrow, anchored on concrete sinks: eval(), the shell-exec family called with a variable argument (command injection), unserialize() (object injection), extract() over a request superglobal (variable injection), and SQL built by interpolation/concatenation into a query call. Method-call lookbehind keeps PDO::exec and similar database methods out of the command-exec rule.

rules.//: eval() executes an arbitrary PHP string at runtime — there is essentially no safe production use with any non-constant input, and it is the canonical RCE primitive. `\beval` requires a word boundary so `medieval(` / `retrieval(` do not match, and `\s*\(` requires it be a call.
rules.message: eval() runs an arbitrary PHP string — a remote-code-execution sink. Remove it; use a real parser, a whitelist/dispatch table, or json_decode for data. There is no safe eval of untrusted input.

rules.//: system/exec/passthru/shell_exec/popen/proc_open invoke a shell; when any argument contains a PHP variable (`$`) the command line is attacker-influenceable — command injection. The trailing `[^)]*\$` requires a variable somewhere in the argument list, so `system("ls -la")` (constant) does NOT match. The leading `(?<![>:])` lookbehind excludes method calls (`$pdo->exec($sql)`, `Foo::exec($x)`) so PDO/mysqli `exec` is not a false positive — only the bare global shell functions match. Fix: escapeshellarg/escapeshellcmd each part, or avoid the shell with proc_open + an argv array and no shell interpretation.
rules.message: Shell exec function (system/exec/passthru/shell_exec/popen/proc_open) called with a variable argument — command injection if the value is user-influenced. escapeshellarg() every interpolated part, or drop the shell entirely and pass an argv array. A bare constant command (no $) is fine; this fires only on a variable.

rules.//: unserialize() on attacker-controlled data is PHP object injection: __wakeup/__destruct gadget chains in loaded classes can be triggered to reach RCE/file-write. WARN because unserialize of trusted, internally-produced data is legitimate. Fix: use json_decode for external data, or unserialize($data, ['allowed_classes' => false]) to forbid object instantiation.
rules.message: unserialize() can trigger PHP object-injection gadget chains (__wakeup/__destruct) when fed untrusted input. Prefer json_decode for external data; if unserialize is required, pass ['allowed_classes' => false] (or an explicit allowlist) and confirm the source is trusted.

rules.//: extract() over a request superglobal ($_GET/$_POST/$_REQUEST/$_COOKIE) creates local variables straight from user input, letting a request overwrite any in-scope variable (auth flags, config, file paths) — variable injection. `extract($config)` on internal data does not match (only the superglobal forms do). WARN: a deliberate, EXTR_SKIP-guarded use exists, but it is almost always a bug — read the specific keys you need explicitly instead.
rules.message: extract() on a request superglobal injects user-controlled variables into local scope and can clobber existing variables — a classic variable-injection bug. Read the specific keys you need ($_POST['name']) explicitly instead of extracting the whole array.

rules.//: SQL passed to ->query() / mysql_query() / mysqli_query() with a PHP variable interpolated into the double-quoted string, or concatenated onto it (`". $var`), is SQL injection. `"[^"]*(?:\$|"\s*\.\s*\$)` matches both the interpolation form (`"... $id"`) and the concat form (`"..." . $id`). A bound query `->query("... = ?")` (closing quote not followed by $ or `. $`) does NOT match. Note: the procedural mysqli_query($conn, "...") form (connection first) is out of scope by design — this rule targets the SQL-string-first shapes; rely on review for the connection-first variant.
rules.message: SQL string with an interpolated/concatenated PHP variable passed to a query call — SQL injection. Use prepared statements with bound parameters (PDO::prepare + execute([...]), or mysqli prepare/bind_param), never variables inside the SQL string.
